meta.rule: This file is the ONLY authorised source for mnemonics and memory aids across all exhibits. No exhibit or script may define mnemonics inline. All Mnemonics tabs must fetch and render exclusively from this file. Additions require deliberate manual editing of this file — not generation by any exhibit build process.

universal_skeleton.label: SA1 Universal Exam Skeleton — 8 Dimensions
universal_skeleton.description: Apply to EVERY exam question regardless of product or topic. Scan all 8 in order. Write on each dimension that the question touches. Skip dimensions that are genuinely out of scope.

dimensions.label: Customer Need
dimensions.prompt: Why does this exist? Who buys it and why?
sub: Individual customer need
Employer / group need
Societal / State gap being filled
Self-employed / specific segment needs

dimensions.label: Benefit Design
sub: Benefit amount and limits (replacement ratio / sum insured)
Benefit timing (deferred period / survival period / expiry)
Trigger / incapacity definition
Exclusions and conditions covered
Escalation / inflation linkage
Options and variations (ROP, waiver, partial benefit)

dimensions.label: Underwriting
dimensions.prompt: Rating factors · Medical triggers · Group: FCL and actively-at-work
sub: Key risk factors and corresponding rating factors
Individual UW: medical / non-medical thresholds
Group UW: free cover limit, actively-at-work, compulsory membership
Anti-selection controls
UW at claim stage (claims underwriting)

dimensions.label: Pricing
dimensions.prompt: Model · Data · Assumptions · Guaranteed vs reviewable
sub: Pricing approach / model (inception-annuity, multi-state, frequency-severity)
Key assumptions (morbidity/inception, recovery, lapse, expenses)
Data availability and credibility
Guaranteed vs reviewable premium structure
Profit testing and capital return requirement

dimensions.label: Claims Management
dimensions.prompt: Trigger · Benefit limitation · Moral hazard · Rehabilitation · In-claim monitoring
sub: Definition of insured event at claim stage
Benefit limitation formula (IP) / condition verification (CI) / pre-authorisation (PMI)
Moral hazard identification and control
Rehabilitation and return-to-work support
Ongoing claim monitoring and medical certification
Fraud detection

dimensions.label: Monitoring & Experience
dimensions.prompt: Why monitor · What to investigate · How · Use of results
sub: Why monitor (actuarial control cycle, pricing update, EV, regulatory)
What to investigate (inception rates, recovery rates, lapses, expenses)
How to investigate (A vs E, exposure calculation, investigation period)
Use of results (re-price new business, improve in-force, reserve basis, EV assumptions)

dimensions.label: Risks
dimensions.prompt: Insurer financial · Operational · Policyholder behaviour · External
sub: Insurer financial risks (morbidity, pricing, reserving, capital)
Operational risks (systems, claims handling, distribution, fraud)
Policyholder behaviour risks (anti-selection, moral hazard, selective lapsing)
External risks (economic cycle, State benefit changes, medical advances, demographic trends, regulation)

dimensions.label: Regulatory & Capital
dimensions.prompt: Solvency · TCF / Consumer Duty · Reporting · Product governance
sub: Solvency and capital requirements (Solvency II / IRDAI equivalent)
Treating Customers Fairly / Consumer Duty obligations
Regulatory reporting requirements
Product governance and target market review
Local IRDAI-specific rules (India context)

aids.title: Three Criteria for CI Condition Inclusion

items.detail: Perceived as serious and occurs frequently enough to price and generate meaningful claims
Precise medical criteria; no ambiguity at claim time; reduces disputes and windfall payments
Enough data to price accurately; reinsurer help if own data insufficient

aids.title: Adverse Effects of High Lapse Rates in CI

items.detail: Healthier lives lapse → worse remaining risk pool → premium spiral
Initial expenses and commission not yet recouped from in-force premiums
Forced sale of assets at unfavourable prices (e.g. recession)
More expensive per remaining policy as portfolio shrinks
Fixed costs spread over fewer policies → higher per-policy expenses
Extra commission earned on policyholder re-entry at lower new-business premium
Reduces capital strain and peak claims exposure
Reinsurer validates assumptions and provides data
Irons out random fluctuations in claims
Ability to write larger risks and more volume
Reduces initial capital required for new business
Condition definitions, UW guidance, claims assessment support
Links with employer sick pay; shorter = higher inception rate; major rating factor
Must stay <100% net of tax; 1% rise ≈ 1% extra claim cost; core moral hazard control
Own occ → suited occ → any occ; changing definition after 2 years reduces long-term moral hazard
Short-term A&S, MPPI, group IP, unit-linked; each has different UW/claims complexity
Benefits linked to RPI/NAEI; protects real value; also escalates premium; subject to max cap
X% of pre-disability income minus all continuing income; monetary maximum for high earners
Definition becomes more restrictive after (e.g.) 2 years; reduces long-term claim incentive
Counselling; vocational retraining; proportionate benefit on partial return — reduces claim duration
Part-time return or lower-paid role partially compensated; avoids cliff-edge loss of full benefit
Regular medical re-certification; insurer-checked not employer; income verification; claims monitoring
Where legislation permits; different morbidity patterns; growing markets often restrict this factor
Inception rates rise with age; claim duration patterns also age-dependent; the main driver
Higher ratio = higher cost; geographic/zone variation significant; India: zone-based pricing expected
THE single most critical IP rating factor; affects both inception probability AND claim duration
Mix shift to mental health/stress; long-duration causes; change in claim type distribution
Selective lapsing → healthier lives leaving; check by duration, channel, benefit level
Early claims by notification date; abnormal large group schemes; new business mix changes
Study separately — actions differ; inception rise vs recovery fall have different solutions
Demographic shift; cross-subsidy breakdown; occupation class drift; scheme size changes

PMI.label: Private Medical Insurance — Frozen Memory Aids

items.detail: Insured retains a share of costs; deductible means insurer pays only above threshold — reduces small claims and moral hazard
Restricts cover to network providers (HMO) or negotiates preferential rates with preferred providers (PPO)
Caps total insurer exposure per year or per benefit category; breaks true indemnity but controls catastrophic cost
Rewards low claimers; reduces frequency of small discretionary claims
GP referral required before specialist / hospital access — prevents unnecessary specialist use
Pre-existing conditions, cosmetic surgery, pregnancy, experimental drugs — reduces adverse selection and uninsurable risk
Pre-existing conditions; moratorium or full medical underwriting applied at entry
Primary driver of claim frequency and cost; older lives claim more and for higher amounts
Where permitted by regulation; males and females have different claim patterns and costs
Higher excess → lower premium; policyholder retains more risk

aids.title: Why PMI Claims Are Hard to Control

items.detail: Insured uses private care instead of State care because PMI exists; increases utilisation above uninsured population
No fixed sum insured — insurer pays actual costs; no natural ceiling; very large individual claims possible
Healthcare inflation consistently exceeds general price inflation; new drugs and procedures add cost
Claims notified weeks or months after treatment; hard to reserve accurately on short-term renewals
Growing awareness of right to claim; encouraged by no-fault culture and broker advice

LTCI.label: Long-Term Care Insurance — Frozen Memory Aids

items.detail: Guaranteed premiums and benefits add to capital requirements; limited guarantee periods mitigate this
Longer lifespans → longer claim durations and higher total claim cost; stroke survivors surviving into care need
Index-linked or indemnity benefits need matching investment returns; low-rate environments squeeze margins
ADL definitions (e.g. 'adequate' washing) are subjective; claims management disputes; fraud risk if benefit paid direct to care provider
Pre-funded: if claims commence before sufficient premiums received, early shortfall risk is acute
Unlimited care costs can rapidly erode all assets including the family home
LTCI preserves wealth above means-test threshold; maintains dignity and choice of care provider
Geographic mobility reduces availability of informal family care; formal care now needed and costly
State provision is means-tested or absent; LTCI fills the gap and avoids asset depletion to qualify for State support
Premiums paid in advance over years; usually reviewable; waiver of premium on claim commencement
Capped total amount or benefit period — cheaper; policyholder retains tail risk
Single premium at point of care need; guaranteed income for life; protects against longevity risk
Optional lump sum if death occurs before or shortly after claim commences
Benefit rises with inflation or care cost index; protects real value of cover over long claim durations

aids.title: Employer Needs for Group Health Cover

items.detail: Fulfil legal sick pay requirements; avoid regulatory penalties
Competitive employee benefit; especially valuable where State provision is limited
Reduce sickness absence duration via PMI / IP; protect business continuity and productivity
Protect against financial loss from long-term absence of key staff
Group cover is cheaper per head than individual; tax-efficient vehicle for employee benefits

aids.title: Bundling vs Unbundling — Who Prefers What

items.detail: Demonstrates value by sourcing best of breed; maintains relationships with multiple insurers; earns commission on each separately
Higher income per customer; lower per-policy expenses; pricing offsets between stable and uncertain benefits
Simpler; one proposal form; lower total cost if package meets needs; no need for advice
Re-price new business using revised morbidity, recovery, lapse and expense assumptions
Update in-force and IBNR / RBNS reserves to reflect actual emerging experience
Identify anti-selection by duration, channel, sum insured; tighten or relax underwriting questions accordingly
Board and senior management decisions on product mix, capital, reinsurance and distribution strategy
Feeds back into assumptions for EV, business planning and regulatory capital modelling

PRICING.label: Pricing & Product Design — Frozen Memory Aids

items.detail: Gather own experience; check credibility; identify gaps; source market/reinsurer data
Morbidity, recovery, lapse, expenses, investment return — best estimate with margins
Allow for select effects, waiting periods, anti-selection at early durations
Apply inception rates × claim cost; use multi-state / frequency-severity model as appropriate
Add expense loading, commission, profit margin, reinsurance cost
Project cashflows; check IRR / NPV meets hurdle rate; stress test key assumptions
Ensure compliance; check market positioning; get sign-off
Compare actual vs expected; trigger re-price or assumption review if outside tolerance
Start with own experience; burning cost = frequency × severity; allow for maturity effects
Proportional vs non-proportional; capital relief; reinsurer as data and technical partner
Systems, claims workflows, TPA vs in-house; setup costs must be priced in
Channel choice drives expenses, persistency and anti-selection profile
Front-end vs back-end; FCL for groups; medical thresholds; legal restrictions on rating factors
Definition of trigger; pre-auth; moral hazard controls; rehabilitation; fraud detection
Product classification (short vs long-term); policyholder tax treatment; regulatory capital

aids.title: Reasons Why Pricing Assumptions May Be Wrong

items.detail: Business written differs from that assumed — occupation class, age band, channel
Economic downturn, medical advances, State benefit changes alter claim experience
Thin cells, short investigation periods, non-credible own experience — wrong base rates
Policy conditions, deferred periods, benefit caps changed but assumptions not updated
Gather policy and claims records; fix duplicates, IBNR/IBNER; choose investigation period
Subdivide by age, gender, duration, occupation, cause — each cell genuinely similar
Central or initial ETR; adjust for partial years, withdrawals, new entrants
Choose benchmark (standard table or pricing basis); compute A/E ratios per cell
Look across a longer window; cross-reference industry/population data; identify persistent trends
Derive best-estimate rates from A/E; smooth sparse cells; use analogous data for novel products
Apply improvement factors; allow for medical advances, care pathway changes, population ageing
Add prudential margin; larger where data is thin or product is novel; state margin explicitly
Quota share crystallises future profits; funds initial outgo; reduces regulatory capital strain
Caps individual and aggregate exposure; smooths year-on-year profit volatility
UW manuals, claims training, IT software, overseas market knowledge, product development data
Ceded risk reduces SCR; reinsurer may operate under lower-cost capital regime (regulatory arbitrage)
Entry into unfamiliar products or territories — reinsurer shares risk and provides local expertise

aids.title: Types of Reinsurance — All Six Forms

items.detail: Each risk assessed and reinsured individually; used for unusual/large risks outside standard treaty terms (e.g. very high IP sum for a key employee)
Proportional — same fixed % of all risks ceded; most common health RI form; used for new product entry, financial strain relief, capacity
Proportional — retention fixed per risk, ceded proportion varies; NOT applicable to short-term health insurance (lacks large/varying individual sums at risk)
Non-proportional — caps claims above a defined retention; can operate per risk, aggregate, or catastrophe basis

items.word: Stop Loss (≡ Aggregate XL in health)
items.detail: Non-proportional — in health insurance, Stop Loss and Aggregate XL are used interchangeably: both cap total claims / loss ratios for a whole account over a period
Standing contract — many risks covered automatically under one arrangement; cheaper and simpler than facultative; avoids adverse selection by reinsurer
Reinsurer aggregates data across many cedants; available on 'no-names' basis; may need adjustment for differences in mix, geography, UW level
Provides UW manuals, acceptance criteria, loadings tables; assists claims assessment, training and monitoring
Proposal-processing systems; claims acceptance tools; reserve-setting software; internet-hosted for instant updates
Reinsurer brings expertise in new products and new markets; helps launch and price products the insurer could not attempt alone
Local policy documents, local contacts (distributors, hospitals, TPAs), local risk statistics, legal/regulatory guidance
Staff training in credibility pricing, IBNR, expense bases; more valuable in health than life because health experience emerges quickly
If tax treatment differs between cedant and reinsurer, quota share can improve combined after-tax result; future rule changes are a risk
Reinsurer may hold less capital than insurer for the same risk; saves on cost of capital — shared via cheaper premiums or higher commission
Both parties benefit: insurer gains experience and in-force; reinsurer gains profitable quota share, diversification and enhanced credibility
Health results more volatile than life; reinsurance smooths year-on-year outcomes — the 'peace of mind' benefit is worth the cost
Prescribed by regulator (e.g. Solvency II SCR/MCR); defines minimum to avoid intervention
Internal assessment based on company's own risk profile and risk appetite; may be more or less than regulatory
Standard set by rating agencies; drives cost of raising external capital and market confidence

aids.title: Methods of Managing the Capital Position

items.detail: Transfers risk; reduces SCR and risk margin; financial RI crystallises future profits
Raises new available capital; most expensive (lowest-ranking); requires credible business plan
Put options, interest rate swaps; reduce SCR by hedging market risk exposures
Debt that ranks below policyholders; counts as Tier 2 own funds under Solvency II
Reduces mismatch risk; lowers interest rate and inflation capital charges
Sells future expected profits to capital markets; less effective under Solvency II
Reduce risk concentrations; outsource expense risk; diversify product/geographic mix
Insurer may collapse before claims fall due; policyholder cannot easily assess insurer strength
Prevent mis-selling, fraud, and incompetent management from harming consumers
Systemic risk — failure of large insurers damages broader financial system
Ensure clear communications, suitability, fair claims handling, no mis-selling

aids.title: Regulatory Responsibilities of a Health Insurance Regulator

items.detail: Permission to write each class of business; pre-approval of novel products in some jurisdictions
Senior management systems and controls; market conduct rules; TCF obligations
Regular standardised returns; balance sheet, revenue account, capital requirements
Assessment visits; review of governance reports; risk-based supervision intensity
Rules for asset and liability valuation; derivation of SCR, MCR and own funds
Vetting of senior roles; ongoing assessment; statutory actuarial appointments
Range from warnings to closure; proportionate to risk; ladder of intervention

aids.title: Features of Health & Care Liabilities Affecting Asset Choice

items.detail: Liabilities mainly domestic; overseas risks need matching currency assets
PMI: very short-term; IP/LTCI: medium to very long — drives duration of assets needed
Indemnity (PMI) and index-linked benefits need real assets or index-linked bonds
Fixed monetary liabilities → fixed-interest assets; real liabilities → index-linked or equities
PMI claims volatile and seasonal; need liquid assets even if yield is lower
Assets backing MCR: safest short-dated government bonds; free assets: scope for equities/property
Duration of asset must match liability mean term
Must be sufficient to meet liability outgo and generate profit
Income and capital value volatility must be acceptable given free asset buffer
Ability to sell quickly without loss; critical for volatile PMI claims
Risk of default; government bonds safest; corporate bonds carry credit risk
Match domestic liabilities with domestic currency assets
Regulatory rules may restrict which assets count toward solvency capital

GBE.label: General Business Environment — Frozen Memory Aids

aids.title: Key Drivers of Demand for Health Insurance

items.detail: Rising incomes increase willingness and ability to pay for supplementary cover
Employer benefits reduce individual demand; absence of employer cover increases it
Comprehensive State system suppresses demand; means-testing or gaps increase it
Price comparison sites; digital distribution; data science enabling new product design
Downturn reduces savings demand but increases protection demand; employment affects IP claims
Ageing population; longer survival in ill-health; fewer children per worker — the time bomb
Full medical underwriting at application; insured knows exactly what is/isn't covered; slower
Front-end controls it explicitly; back-end relies on moratorium exclusions — less precise
Immediate cover without underwriting; pre-existing conditions excluded at claim stage for defined period; faster but creates uncertainty for policyholder

STR.label: Strategy & Overseas Markets — Frozen Memory Aids

aids.title: Factors to Assess When Entering an Overseas Market

items.detail: Level and type — domestic giants, multinationals, TPAs, community funds, State quasi-monopoly
Directly drives demand for PMI and other products; varies enormously by jurisdiction
Local sales structures; remuneration regulation; need for local partner or JV
Translation; local contacts (hospitals, consultants); religious/cultural product adaptations
Local law; contract enforceability; regulatory approval process; equality legislation
Reinsurer as market-entry partner; data, UW manuals, technical assistance, local contacts
Understand motivation; hostile vs friendly determines data access and speed
H&C needs far more data than life; willing seller opens books; hostile provides only public data
Time-limited; treat projections with caution; sensitivity test key assumptions
Research past comparable deals; calibrate price and avoid repeating mistakes
Independent actuarial review; ring-fence uncertain liabilities if needed
Verify synergy claims carefully; confirm capital adequacy after transfer; check monopoly rules

IFRS17.label: IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117 — Profit Reporting (Chapter 14) — Frozen Memory Aids

items.detail: PV of probability-weighted future net cashflows. Can be negative for profitable regular-premium contracts.
Compensation for non-market / non-financial risk. Calculated via VaR, CTE, or CoC. Confidence level: insurer's choice — not specified by IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117.
Unearned / future profit. Never negative. Released over coverage period using coverage units (e.g. policies in-force). Annual cohort grouping applies.
Default for all contracts. Also called BBA (Building Block Approach). Used for long-term health: term assurance, IP, CI, without-profit annuities.
Mandatory for direct participation features (unit-linked, with-profits / DPF). CSM is unlocked when discount rate changes — absorbs the gain/loss rather than hitting P&L.
Simplified model for short-term contracts (<1yr). Similar to unearned premium reserve. NOT covered further in SA1 subject.

aids.title: Three Criticisms of IFRS 4 — Solved by IFRS 17

items.detail: Profits were highly sensitive to reserving assumptions; small changes caused large P&L swings. IFRS 17 fix: CSM buffers assumption changes.
Even within a single country, insurers used incomparable methodologies. IFRS 17 fix: single principle-based global standard.
Profit recognised at inception for contracts providing 20-30 years of coverage. IFRS 17 fix: CSM defers profit to be released over the coverage period.

aids.title: Three Transition Approaches — In Order of Preference

items.detail: Preferred. Use if practicable. Apply Ind AS 117 as if it always existed — complete historical look-back. India: at least 10yr in FY27, 15yr in FY28, 20yr in FY29.
If FRA not practicable. Simplified version using available reasonable and supportable information. Achieves closest outcome to FRA.
Last resort. CSM = Fair value of policies minus Fulfilment cashflows at transition date. No historical data required.
If BEL + RA exceeds initial net cashflow, the shortfall is recognised immediately as a loss. CSM floors at zero — cannot be negative at inception or subsequently.
Changes to non-investment assumptions (e.g. morbidity rates) adjust the CSM first, not P&L. Stabilises reported profit. Only if CSM insufficient does the shortfall hit P&L.
Written down over the policy term in line with a chosen coverage unit measure (e.g. number of policies in-force). Contracts grouped in annual cohorts — policies >1yr apart cannot be grouped.
CSM = (Initial premium − Initial expenses) − (BEL + RA). This captures exactly the profit that would otherwise have been recognised on day one under older standards.
Quantile of the loss distribution. Confidence level chosen by insurer (not specified by IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117). Simpler to calculate.
Also called CVaR or Tail VaR. Average loss beyond the VaR threshold. Captures the tail more fully than VaR. Preferred by many actuaries.
Similar to Solvency II risk margin. RA = PV of cost of holding required capital over the remaining coverage period. Familiar to actuaries.

aids.title: Eight IFRS Principles (IAS 1)
aids.phrase: Fair Gorillas Are Clearly Correct, Reporting Under Orders

items.detail: Statements must present fairly the financial position, performance and cash flows. The 'true and fair view' requirement.
Entity assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future. Opposite = wind-up / break-up basis — a frequently-tested exam distinction.
Revenue recorded when earned; expenses when incurred — regardless of when cash is received or paid.
Presentation and classification retained from period to period unless a change is justified by changed circumstances or a new IFRS.
Explicit and unreserved statement of compliance required if claiming IFRS compliance. Must comply with ALL requirements. US GAAP does NOT require this explicit statement.
Presumed at least annual. If period changes, entity must disclose reason and state that amounts are not entirely comparable.
Assets and liabilities, income and expenses, generally may NOT be offset. Under US GAAP, offsetting of derivatives is optional; under IFRS it is required when stated conditions are met.
Financial information should be understandable to users who have knowledge of business, economic activity and accounting. Complexity is not a reason to exclude information.

aids.title: GMM vs VFA — The Critical Comparison
aids.phrase: VFA Locks the CSM; GMM Hits P&L

items.word: VFA — CSM is unlocked on discount rate change
items.detail: Under VFA, discount rate references the underlying asset pool. When the rate changes, BEL and RA change, and the CSM is adjusted (unlocked) to absorb the change — it does not hit P&L immediately.

items.word: Locks profit in CSM for future release
items.detail: The VFA CSM absorbs rate movements and releases them gradually over the contract term. Lower P&L volatility for unit-linked and with-profits business.

items.word: GMM — CSM NOT unlocked on discount rate change
items.detail: Under GMM, the discount rate is derived from market-observable data. When the rate rises, BEL and RA fall — the gain goes straight to P&L (not captured in CSM).
Higher P&L volatility under GMM because interest rate movements pass through directly. Appropriate for traditional non-participating business (term assurance, IP, CI, annuities).

aids.title: Ind AS 117 vs IFRS 17 — Five Specific Differences
aids.phrase: DATE-C — How Ind AS 117 departs from IFRS 17

items.detail: Ind AS 117: annual periods beginning on/after 1 April 2024. IFRS 17: 1 January 2023. IRDAI mandating 1 April 2026 for Indian insurers.
Early adoption under Ind AS 117 is permitted only for consolidation purposes by a parent/investor/venturer. Under IFRS 17, general early adoption is permitted for entities that already apply IFRS 9.
IFRS 17 para C28C references IAS 39 for impairment in transition. In Ind AS 117, this is replaced with Ind AS 109 provisions (India never issued an Ind AS equivalent of IAS 39).
IFRS 17 scope exclusion cites IAS 26 (Retirement Benefit Plans). Ind AS 117 retains the exclusion substance but omits the IAS 26 reference — no Ind AS equivalent to IAS 26 has been notified in India.
IFRS 3 requires gain on bargain purchase in P&L. Ind AS 103 requires it as Capital Reserve. Paragraphs B95A and B95C of Ind AS 117 amended accordingly.

aids.title: IGAAP to Ind AS — Seven Key Changes
aids.phrase: DREAM OI — what changes when India moves to Ind AS

items.word: Discounting allowed for General Insurance liabilities
items.detail: IGAAP: discounting of GI liabilities not permitted. Ind AS 117: discounting required to reflect time value of money. Reduces liability values; affects profit pattern.
IGAAP: reinsurance netted against insurance liabilities. Ind AS 117: insurance liabilities and reinsurance assets presented separately (gross). Balance sheet appears larger.
IGAAP: incurred loss model (impairment recognised when loss event occurs). Ind AS 109: Expected Credit Loss model — forward-looking, probability-weighted. Earlier recognition of credit losses.
IGAAP: acquisition costs expensed immediately. Ind AS 117: amortised over coverage period via the CSM mechanism. Smoother expense recognition; removes front-loading distortion.
IGAAP: prudential actuarial valuation per IRDAI Regulations. Ind AS 117: BEL (discounted at current market rates) + RA + CSM. Best estimate assumptions — not prudential.
IGAAP: onerous contracts not explicitly identified. Ind AS 117: if BEL + RA exceeds initial net cashflow at inception, loss recognised immediately. CSM cannot be negative.
IGAAP: IRDAI prescribes valuation (debt at historical cost, equity at fair value). Ind AS 109: FVPL / FVOCI / Amortised Cost based on business model test and SPPI test.

aids.title: India Implementation Key Facts
aids.phrase: Follow the MAPS to Ind AS

items.detail: G.S.R. 492(E) notified Ind AS 117. Effective from 1 April 2024 in principle. MCA vide G.S.R. 602(E) dated 28 Sep 2024 granted transitional relief — insurers may use Ind AS 104 for consolidation by parent until IRDAI notifies.
IRDAI proposes mandatory adoption for Life, General, Health and Reinsurers from 1 April 2026. Year 1: parallel IGAAP submission + independent validation from IRDAI empanelled auditor.
Phase 1 (listed or AUM>Rs 35,000cr): 15 insurers. Phase 2 (AUM Rs 10,000-35,000cr + all FRBs): 20. Phase 3 (AUM ≤ Rs 10,000cr): 26. All have submitted gap assessments and FY23-24 proforma statements.

Distributable surplus for participating business remains determined by actuarial valuation under Insurance Act Section 49. Shareholder share capped at 10% of actuarial surplus. Ind AS 117 accounting profit ≠ distributable surplus — reconciliation disclosure required.

items.word: Cannot group contracts issued >1 year apart
items.detail: Para 22 of Ind AS 117: contracts issued more than one year apart cannot be included in the same group. Core principle — prevents cross-subsidisation across policy generations.
Each cohort further divided into: (i) onerous at inception, (ii) no significant possibility of becoming onerous, (iii) other profitable contracts (Para 16).

FY 2026-27: at least 10 years retrospective. FY 2027-28: at least 15 years. FY 2028-29: at least 20 years. Para B70 flexibility: changes identifiable at higher aggregation level may be allocated systematically to cohort groups.
Para C10 transition relief: not required to divide groups into annual cohorts at transition if impracticable. Must apply annual cohort requirement prospectively after transition date.

items.word: EU optional exemption — India did NOT adopt
items.detail: EU Regulation 2021/2036 introduced an optional exemption for intergenerationally-mutualised participating contracts (subject to IASB PIR review by 31 Dec 2027). India: no exemption — full compliance required.

aids.title: IFRS 9 / Ind AS 109 Asset Classification
aids.phrase: Full Freedom At the choice — FVPL, FVOCI, Amortised

items.word: Fair Value through Profit and Loss
items.detail: Default basis. Changes in fair value recognised in P&L as they arise. Most volatile effect on reported profit. Replaces IGAAP mix of historical cost (debt) and fair value (equity).

items.word: Fair Value through Other Comprehensive Income
items.detail: Where restrictive criteria met. Fair value changes bypass P&L and go to OCI (unrealised gains/losses). Used for some debt instruments and equity investments designated at election.

Asset held at initial amount adjusted for repayments, interest and amortisation. Applicable when business model = hold to collect contractual cash flows AND cash flows are solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI test).

aids.title: US GAAP Unique Concepts — Not in IFRS

items.detail: Notional asset equal to deferrable acquisition costs, if recoverable from future premiums or margins. Amortised over policy term as margins emerge. Avoids front-loading of expense distortion to reported profit.

US GAAP values assets at acquisition cost rather than fair value (reliability over relevance). Moving toward fair values but more slowly than IFRS. Choice of accounting basis affects pace of profit recognition, not total profit.

Reserve set up when premium-paying period is shorter than insurance period. Offsets excess profits being recognised during the premium-paying period and releases (amortises) over the insurance period. Spreads profit recognition.

aids.title: Two P&L Lines Mandated by Ind AS 117
aids.phrase: Service and Finance — never mix them

items.detail: = Insurance Revenue (incl. CSM release) minus Insurance Service Expenses (claims, expenses, loss components). Pure underwriting / operational performance. Unaffected by market rate movements.

Changes in the carrying amount of insurance liabilities due to discount rate changes and unwinding of the time value of money. Separates market / financial effects from underwriting performance. Under IGAAP these were mixed — a key source of profit volatility.

RISKS.label: Risks Facing a Health Insurer — DAPPER C Framework

aids.title: Main Risks Facing a Health Insurer
aids.phrase: Dirty Assumptions Price Policies Exposing Regulated Companies

items.detail: Before anything else, you need accurate data — garbage in, garbage out.
sub: Incomplete / inaccurate policy data held on policyholders
Assumption data not applicable to the purpose for which it is required
Non-disclosure / misrepresentation / fraud by policyholder (especially under moratorium approach)

items.detail: Even with good data, your model and its parameters can still be wrong — and outcomes fluctuate randomly.
sub: Model risk: underlying model structure is inadequate
Parameter risk: parameters chosen for the model are incorrect
Random fluctuation risk: unpredictable sample error around the mean
Scope of assumptions: inception rates, mortality, recovery, claim frequency/severity, expenses, expense inflation, lapses/withdrawals, volume and mix of new business
items.sub_mnemonic: MICE-WN — Mortality, Inception, Claims, Expenses, Withdrawals (lapses), New business (volume & mix)

items.detail: Competitive pressure pushes you to cut rates or add guarantees you cannot afford.
sub: Pressure to reduce premium rates below sustainable levels
Risk of mis-pricing embedded guarantees and options
Management accepting unacceptable risks to grow size of business or maximise short-term shareholder earnings

items.detail: The policy itself and external medical developments can bite you.
sub: Policy wording risk: claims paid that were never intended to be covered
Advances in medical science / early diagnosis (e.g. national screening programmes) increasing claims
Catastrophes such as epidemics and wars

items.detail: Everyone you deal with outside the company can let you down.
sub: Reinsurers: failure of a reinsurer under reinsurance arrangements
Distributors: committing insurer to new conditions, delaying premium/claim payments, bankruptcy, reputational damage
Medical service providers: influence over claims costs and the client relationship

items.detail: The rules of the game can change overnight.
sub: Tax changes affecting pricing or policyholder behaviour
Changes to policy conditions required by regulation
Premium rating regulation
Aggregation and concentration risk from over-specialisation of product or over-concentration of distribution

items.detail: Internal failures sink ships.
sub: Internal audit failures and internal fraud
Customer service shortcomings and reputational risk
Physical risks: fire, flood, IT recovery failure, loss of key staff
Aggregation / concentration of risk from product specialisation or distributor over-reliance

aids.title: Complete Underwriting Framework

items.detail: 4 As — Anti-selection prevention; Allocation to homogeneous risk groups; Avoidance of over-insurance; Alignment of actual experience with pricing assumptions

Full medical UW (evidence at entry; long-term products IP/CI); Moratorium (no UW at entry; PECs excluded for 2-3 clean years; check at claim; suited to PMI/group CI); MHD/disregarded (no exclusions; greatest anti-selection risk; common in group PMI)
extras: NWT — No Worse Terms at renewal
CPME — NWT with original exclusions only
Moratorium: 5yr lookback; 2-3yr consecutive clean period; clock resets if treatment received

items.detail: PMMS + F — Proposal form; Primary Medical Attendant Report (PMAR); Medical examination; Specialist tests; Financial underwriting (caps benefit to replacement ratio)
extras: Non-medical risk factors: occupation/class, leisure pursuits, country of residence, overseas travel
Non-medical limits: escalating evidence thresholds by sum-at-risk and age
Psychosocial factors relevant for IP: GP attendance patterns, work absence history, adverse life events

items.detail: LEAD + P — Load (higher premium); Exclude (named condition excluded); Accept at standard terms; Decline (last resort); Postpone (temporary elevated risk)
extras: Additional tools: reduced benefit; alternative product; 100% facultative reinsurance
Misrepresentation: Innocent=pay in full; Careless=proportionate remedy; Deliberate=void from inception
Non-contestability clause: after ~2 years on risk, cannot void for innocent/careless misrepresentation

items.detail: FAME — Free Cover Limit (no individual UW below threshold); Actively at work clause; Moratorium/MHD preferred for group; Experience assessed at group average
extras: Group anti-selection controls: minimum scheme size; minimum take-up rate; compulsory membership; benefits linked to salary formula
Individual: full medical UW for IP/CI/LTCI; moratorium standard for PMI; greater anti-selection risk

exam_angles: Online/simplified UW — speed/cost savings but higher non-disclosure, anti-selection, reinsurer renegotiation risks
Automated/tele-UW — consistency and cost savings but no human judgement; underwriters lose skill on routine cases
Claims underwriting — check at claim stage for non-disclosure even with full UW at entry
Genetics restrictions — cannot require/use genetic test results; creates anti-selection; may force broader exclusions
Data science in UW — wearables/banking/social media data; benefits: automated UW; risks: privacy, regulatory, algorithmic bias
Reinsurer UW support — UW manuals, training, facultative capacity; vital for new products/markets
